night and it is understood that mat


ters of NOR. than ordinary interest
will be discussed.


Clarence Smith, secretary OF the
American Labor union, is expected
to arrive in the city in a few days.
Mr.. Smith is the editor of the
American Labor Union Journal, of
fetal organ Of the body he repre-
sents.


Mr.. Hughes, organizer for the
American Labor union, says that
the labor movement is going stead
lily forward in Spokane. New
unions are being formed every day
and the old ones are enjoying a
healthy growth. There is no trou-
BILE of Importance at present en-
gaging the unions attention" Mr.
Hughes is now organizing at Priest
River and Newport.


A prominent labor leader stated
last evening that, while he is glad
to see some of the unions endeavOr-
ing to enter into a closer relation
ship to Offset any concerted antagO-
nistic action on the part of other
organizations, yet he felt that there
was no absolute need Of the allied
council that is to be formed. The
trades council, in his opinion, will
make itself felt should injury be
attempted to organized labor in the
city.


The statement about the excess
i've wages in Spokane made by the
contractors who are bidding on the
construction of the warehouse at
Fort Wright does not meet with
the approval of the unions. They
should say something about the
cost of MATERIAL said a union man
this morning, and not call attention
simply to union wages, which are
no higher here than elsewhere. Ma


terial is very high in this city and
/ it has just as much to do with the


rejection of the bids for the con


struction Of the warehouse at Fort
Wright as wages"
